coredata.dc:title: Dracocephalum Moldavica L.-derived carbon dots for effective periodontitis treatment via reprogramming energy metabolism in macrophages
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Bo
Ding, Ruolin
He, Xi
Li, Yifei
He, Zhongshan
Fang, Jie
Guo, Mengran
Wang, Xinchun
Zhao, Zhihe
Song, Xiangrong
coredata.dc:description: Dracocephalum Moldavica L. (DML), a Chinese traditional medicine rich in flavone compounds (e.g., tilianin), has shown antioxidant and anti-inflammatory potential for alleviating inflammatory disorders. However, the extraction of active ingredients of DML is complex, and these extracts usually act synergistically to treat inflammatory diseases. In this study, the carbon dots derived from DML (T-CDs) were rapidly prepared via the one-pot method and showed good performance in biocompatibility and free radical scavenging capacity. Then, the T-CDs were loaded into the hydrogel and applied to treat periodontitis. The in vivo experiments demonstrated that T-CDs could promote the inflammation resolution of and the alveolar bone reparation in the periodontitis model after periodontal injection. With further investigation, it could be found that T-CDs involved the alternative polarization of macrophage by rebalancing glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration. Overall, it was the first time DML had been used to prepare carbon dots with anti-inflammation. This study provided an effective drug candidate for the treatment of periodontitis and offered an in-depth understanding of the therapeutic mechanism of T-CDs in periodontitis through macrophage immunomodulation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dracocephalum Moldavica L.
Carbon dots
Anti-inflammation
Macrophage polarization
Periodontitis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of carbon dots and hydrogel
The drug release property of hydrogel
Biosafety test of T-CDs in vitro
RNA extraction and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction assay
Rat periodontitis model establishment and treatment
Histologic and immunofluorescent staining of tissues
Immunomodulatory effects of T-CDs on macrophage polarization
T-CDs rebalance glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration in macrophages
Hydrogel T-GCDs applied in the treatment of periodontitis in vivo
The therapeutic effects of T-GCDs in vivo
The therapeutic mechanism of T-GCDs in vivo

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Regents: Dracocephalum Moldavica L. was supported by Prof. Xinchun Wang of Shihezi University. Poloxamer 407 and sodium alginate were purchased from Choron Chemicals Co. Ltd. The regent kits used in the antioxidant assays were purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute.

This study is supported by National Key R&D\u00B7Program of China (2023YFC3403200); Sichuan University Postdoctoral Interdisciplinary Innovation Fund (JCXK2203); National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82301115, 32271416); Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, Research and Develop Program, West China Hospital of Stomatology Sichuan University (Grant No. RD-02-202408); the Science and Technology Project of Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps (No. 2022AB020); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2023 M742459).

This study is supported by National Key R&D\u00B7Program of China (2023YFC3403200); Sichuan University Postdoctoral Interdisciplinary Innovation Fund ( JCXK2203 ); National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 82301115 , 32271416 ); Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities , Research and Develop Program, West China Hospital of Stomatology Sichuan University (Grant No. RD-02-202408 ); the Science and Technology Project of Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps (No. 2022AB020 ); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2023\u202F M742459 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: T-CDs prepared from Dracocephalum moldavica L. and its relative hydrogel T-GCDs are applied in the treatment of periodontitis.

The characterization of T-CDs. (A) HTEM images and magnified images of T-CDs particles showing their crystallinity. (B) The hydrodynamic diameters and surface charges of T-CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption and FL emission (Ex: 420 nm) spectrum of T-CDs. (D-G) High-resolution XPS spectra of T-CDs. (H) The fluorescent intensity of dialysis water outside at different times. (I) The free radical scavenging properties of T-CDs at different concentrations.

Intracellular ROS scavenging properties of T-CDs. (A) The ROS levels in macrophages stimulated by H2O2 with the pretreatment of T-CDs observed by confocal microscopy images and cell surface plots. (B) Quantification of fluorescent intensity of intracellular ROS signals via confocal microscopy. (C) Flow cytometric analysis of ROS-positive cells stimulated by H2O2 with the pretreatment of T-CDs. (D) Confocal microscopy images of ROS levels in macrophages stimulated by LPS and IFN-γ with the pretreatment of T-CDs. (E) Quantification of fluorescent intensity of intracellular ROS signals via confocal microscopy. (F) Flow cytometric analysis of ROS-positive cells stimulated by LPS and IFN-γ with the pretreatment of T-CDs. All data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

Immunomodulation of T-CDs to promote M2 macrophage polarization in vitro. (A) qRT-PCR analysis showing relative mRNA expression levels of M1-related genes (Nos2, Tnfα, Il6) and M2-related genes (Arg1) in RAW264.7 cultured in different conditions for 24 h. (B) Flow cytometry analysis showing the percentages of CD86+ (M1 macrophage marker) cells in RAW264.7 cultured in different conditions for 24 h. (C) Quantification of the percentages of CD86+ cells according to flow cytometry. (D) Flow cytometry analysis showing the percentages CD206+ (M2 macrophage marker) cells. (E) Quantification of the percentages of CD206+ cells according to flow cytometry. (F) Immunofluorescent staining images of the expression of iNOS (M1 macrophage marker) and CD206 (M2 macrophage marker) in RAW264.7. Scale bars, 20 µm. (G, H) The quantification of mean fluorescent intensity of iNOS and CD206, respectively. All data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

The immunomodulatory mechanism of T-CDs on macrophage in vitro. (A) A schematic showing the influence of T-CDs on DEGs in the macrophage polarization. (B) Heatmap of DEGs related with the M1 macrophage polarization, M2 macrophage polarization, glycolysis and glutathione synthesis from RNA-seq analysis. (C) Enriched GO terms (biological process terms) of DEGs of macrophages stimulated by LPS/IFN-γ versus those treated by T-CDs under the stimulation of LPS/IFN-γ. (D) Enriched KEGG pathways of DEGs of macrophages in the LPS/IFN-γ group versus the T-CDs group. (E) Enriched Reactome pathways of DEGs of macrophages in the LPS/IFN-γ group versus the T-CDs group.

(A) Representative immunofluorescence images of mitochondrial membrane potential shown by the JC-1 monomers and aggregates in LPS/IFN-γ group and T-CDs group. (B) The glycolytic proton efflux rates (glycoPERs) measured by a XF Seahorse Analyzer. The glycoPERs were normalized by total cell number per group. (C) The OCRs measured by a XF Seahorse Analyzer normalized by total cell number per group. (D, E) Statistical analysis of the normalized glycoPERs and OCRs related to basal glycolysis, compensatory glycolysis, basal respiration, maximal respiration and ATP production. (F) The schematic for the immunomodulatory mechanism exhibited by T-CDs on macrophage. All data represent the mean ± SD (n = 3). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

In vivo therapeutic effects of T-GCDs on periodontitis. (A) Schematic illustration of periodontitis model and dosing regimen. (B) 3D reconstruction images of micro-CT scanning of alveolar bone. The lingual and buccal bone loss areas enclosed between the cemental-enamel junction (CEJ) and alveolar bone crest (ABC) were shaded by red. (C) Quantification of trabecular bone volume per tissue volume (BV/TV) of the region of interest (ROI) at the tooth furcation of the maxillary second molars. (D)Quantification of buccal and lingual bone loss areas. (E, F) H&E staining and TRAP staining images of periodontal tissues. The alveolar bone areas are enclosed by yellow dashed line. R, root; B: bone. (G) Quantification of TRAP+ osteoclasts/bone surface in the alveolar bone around the maxillary second molars. (H) qRT-PCR analysis showing relative mRNA expression levels of M1-related genes (Il1b, Nos2,) and M2-related genes (Arg1, Mrc1) in gingiva around the maxillary second molars. All data represent the mean ± SD (n = 6) *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The immunomodulatory effect of T-GCDs to promote M2 macrophage polarization in vivo. (A) Immunofluorescence staining of CD68 (Mφ marker), iNOS (M1-related marker), and CD163 (M2-related marker) in periodontal tissues. B: bone; P: periodontal ligaments; T: tooth. (B) Quantification of the percentage of the M1 and M2 macrophages among Mφ in periodontal tissues. (C) Quantification of the percentage of the M1 and M2 macrophages among Mφ in CLNs. (D) Quantification of the percentage of the M1 and M2 macrophages among Mφ in the spleen. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 6). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01.

The immunomodulatory mechanism of T-GCDs in vivo. (A) Heatmap showing the DEGs related with the M1 macrophages, M2 macrophages and glycolysis in gingiva from the rats in the groups of control, periodontitis and periodontitis treated by T-GCDs. (B) Enriched GO terms of DEGs of gingiva from the periodontitis group versus T-GCDs group. (C) Enriched KEGG pathways of differentially expressed genes of gingiva from the periodontitis group versus T-GCDs group.

head.ce:title: Dracocephalum Moldavica L.-derived carbon dots for effective periodontitis treatment via reprogramming energy metabolism in macrophages

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dracocephalum Moldavica L. (DML), a Chinese traditional medicine rich in flavone compounds (e.g., tilianin), has shown antioxidant and anti-inflammatory potential for alleviating inflammatory disorders. However, the extraction of active ingredients of DML is complex, and these extracts usually act synergistically to treat inflammatory diseases. In this study, the carbon dots derived from DML (T-CDs) were rapidly prepared via the one-pot method and showed good performance in biocompatibility and free radical scavenging capacity. Then, the T-CDs were loaded into the hydrogel and applied to treat periodontitis. The in vivo experiments demonstrated that T-CDs could promote the inflammation resolution of and the alveolar bone reparation in the periodontitis model after periodontal injection. With further investigation, it could be found that T-CDs involved the alternative polarization of macrophage by rebalancing glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration. Overall, it was the first time DML had been used to prepare carbon dots with anti-inflammation. This study provided an effective drug candidate for the treatment of periodontitis and offered an in-depth understanding of the therapeutic mechanism of T-CDs in periodontitis through macrophage immunomodulation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the sixth most common human disease, periodontitis affects 45 %–50 % of the population worldwide, causing the destruction of periodontal supportive tissues and eventually edentulism [1]. Periodontitis also has a close biological association with various non‐communicable inflammatory disorders [2]. The clinical need for periodontal inflammation control and periodontal tissue repair arises to improve the long-term prognosis of teeth and control the risks of various systemic disorders.

Host immunomodulation therapies have gained great interest as adjunct treatments for periodontitis, in which macrophage-modulated strategies are potent approaches. Macrophages play critical roles in developing and resolving inflammation in periodontitis and are emerging as potential therapeutic targets for periodontitis in various preclinical studies [3–5]. The activation and pro-inflammatory polarization of macrophages is closely modulated by oxidative stress, which results from the imbalance between the generation and elimination of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [6]. This surplus of ROS can elicit the polarization of pro-inflammatory macrophages and aggravate inflammation and tissue destruction. Thus, ROS-scavenging strategies can effectively modulate the inflammatory microenvironment for the treatment of periodontitis.

Dracocephalum Moldavica L. (DML) is a traditional Chinese medicine widely distributed in northwest China. In the Xinjiang Autonomous Region, DML has been used as a folk medicine to treat coronary heart disease, hypertension, and asthma. According to the phytochemical investigations on DML, it was found that DML was rich in flavonoids (e.g., tilianin), triterpenes, phenolic acids and coumarins, which showed anti-inflammation and antioxidant activities [7,8]. However, the active ingredients extraction of DML is complex, and these extracts usually act synergistically in the treatment of inflammatory diseases [9]. On the other hand, DML is not currently used to treat periodontitis, and its therapeutic effect is unclear. Moreover, the therapeutic efficacy of DML or these extracts is primarily limited by their poor water solubility, which urgently needs to be solved for the clinical application of DML.

Carbon dots (CDs), as the emerging class of ultrasmall nanoparticles with a size below 10 nm, have attracted broad research interest for years due to their exclusive water solubility, cell permeability, biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. [10–12]. Because of the incomplete carbonization, some functional groups can be preserved in the structure of CDs, which endows CDs with exclusive functions from precursors [13]. CDs have been proved to be the efficient drug delivery systems or theranostics agents themselves, and widely applied in the treatment of a wide range of diseases [14–18]. According to the previous researches, various natural compounds, such as plant substances, fruit peels and vegetables have been applied as precursors for the synthesis of functional CDs [19,20]. While it has also been proved that the poor water solubility and bioavailability of effective constituent in those compounds were much improved by the preparation of CDs.

Herein, we developed a CDs-based nanoplatform for transforming Chinese medicines DML into biocompatible nanomedicines to treat periodontitis. Firstly, T-CDs were prepared with DML as the precursor via a solvothermal method. To achieve the in-situ administration, T-CDs were loaded into the hydrogel formed with poloxamer 407 (P407) and sodium alginate (SA), then the hydrogel (T-GCDs) was injected into the periodontal pockets of rats (Fig. 1 ). We speculated that the T-CDs could display good water-solubility and showed antioxidant and immunomodulatory effects on macrophage polarization under inflammatory stimuli. This work represented the versatile potential of DML-derived CDs in addressing periodontitis and provided a simple method to efficiently transform Chinese medicine into safe and effective nanomedicine.

Regents: Dracocephalum Moldavica L. was supported by Prof. Xinchun Wang of Shihezi University. Poloxamer 407 and sodium alginate were purchased from Choron Chemicals Co. Ltd. The regent kits used in the antioxidant assays were purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute.

Instruments: The particle sizes and zeta potential were measured by Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The results of transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN), X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (AXIS Supra) were obtained in Analytical & Testing Center of Sichuan University. The fluorescent property and ultraviolet absorption of carbon dots were performed with a Fluorolog-3 (HORIBA) spectrofluorometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of carbon dots and hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The powder of Dracocephalum moldavica L. (5 g) and dry ethanol (30 mL) were added to make a suspension. The mixture was transferred to a reaction kettle at 180 °C for 5 h and then cooled to room temperature naturally. After centrifugation, the supernatant was filtered with 0.45 μm filter membrane filtrate to remove large particles, and the filtrate was concentrated by evaporating to obtain a yellow–brown oil reffered as T-CDs.

Poloxamer 407 (180 mg) and sodium alginate (30 mg) were weighed according to the mass ratio of 6:1, and T-CDs solution (PBS pH 7.4, 5 mg/mL) stored at 4 °C was added. Then the mixture was set in 4 °C overnight to form the uniform and transparent hydrogel. The final concentration of T-CDs in the hydrogel was 5 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The drug release property of hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mL of T-CDs hydrogel was added into a Spectra/Por Float-A-Lyzer (MWCO 0.5–1 kDa) and dialyzed against with 2 mL ultrapure water. Then 1 mL dialysate in outside was used to measure its fluorescence.
The antioxidant activities of T-CDs with different concentrations were evaluated via the reagent kits of ·OH, O2 – and DPPH·.

Mouse macrophage cells RAW264.7 (ATCC) were cultured in high glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle media (DMEM, Hyclone, USA) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (Gibco, USA), 1 % penicillin, and streptomycin (Life Technologies, USA). RAW 264.7 cells (3 × 105 cells/mL) were pretreated by T-CDs (100 μg/mL) for 12 h. The culture medium was removed and cells were stimulated by 100 μM H2O2 (500 μL/mL in culture medium) or 100 ng/mL LPS and IFN-γ (10 ng/mL) for 2 h. The CellROX Deep Red probe (C10422, Invitrogen, USA) was applied to test the intracellular ROS signals. The ROS signals were then observed and measured by CLSM (Olympus, FV3000) and flow cytometry (Attune NxT, ThermoFisher, USA). The group without H2O2 or LPS/IFN-γ stimulation was used as a control.

RAW264.7 (ATCC) were cultured in complete culture medium. The in vitro inflammation was stimulated by lipopolysaccharide (LPS, 100 ng/ml, purified from Escherichia coli, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) and IFN-γ (10 ng/mL, 575302, BioLegend, USA) for 24 h. In the T-CDs group, the T-CDs (100 μg/mL) were added into the culture medium with the stimulus of LPS/ IFN-γ.

Flow cytometry was applied to analyze the phenotypes macrophages. PE conjugated anti-mouse CD86 (BioLegend) and APC conjugated anti-mouse CD206 (BioLegend) were applied for the flow cytometry of macrophage RAW264.7 to test the M1 and M2 phenotypes respectively. Data were further analyzed by the FlowJo software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety test of T-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The biosafety test T-CDs in vitro was investigated by the viability of macrophages and fibroblasts cultured with different concentration of T-CDs for 36 h. The RAW264.7 and L929 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well with 100 μL DMEM complete medium and cultured at 37 °C with 5 % CO2. After culturing for 72 h, the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) or Annexin V, FITC apoptosis detection reagent was applied following the instruction. Colorimetry was then conducted using a plate reader at 450 nm (Thermo MultiskanGO) for CCK-8 test and flow cymetry was conducted for cell apoptosis detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction assay
ce:section.ce:para: We extracted the total RNA from macrophages or rat gingiva using TRIzol (Invitrogen), followed by the synthesis of cDNA from 1 μg total RNA with reverse transcriptase (Takara Biotechnology). The qRT-PCR was then performed using SYBR-Green PCR Master Mix Kit (Takara Biotechnology). All gene expression levels were normalized to glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH). Supplementary table 1 lists the primer sequences used for mouse Nos2, TNFα, Il6, Arg1 and rat Nos2, Il1b, Arg1, Mrc1. The results were calculated following the ΔΔCT method and presented as fold changes relative to GAPDH.

RNA purity was assessed by the NanoPhotometer® spectrophotometer (IMPLEN, CA, USA). RNA concentration was measured by the Qubit® RNA Assay Kit in Qubit® 2.0 Flurometer (Life Technologies, CA, USA). RNA integrity was evaluated by the RNA Nano 6000 Assay Kit of the Bioanalyzer 2100 system (Agilent Technologies, CA, USA). mRNA was purified from total RNA by the poly-T oligo-attached magnetic beads. The first strand cDNA synthesis was conducted using random hexamer primer and M−MuLV Reverse Transcriptase (RNase H-), and the second strand cDNA was subsequently synthesized using DNA Polymerase I and RNase H. The PCR was performed with Phusion High-Fidelity DNA polymerase, Universal PCR primers and Index (X) Primer. The PCR products were purified (AMPure XP system) and library quality was assessed on the Agilent Bioanalyzer 2100 system. The library preparations were sequenced on an Illumina Novaseq platform and 150 bp paired-end reads were generated.

Differential expression analysis was performed via the DESeq2 R package (1.20.0). Genes with adjusted P-value < 0.05 were defined as differentially expressed. GO, KEGG and Reactome enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes were performed by the clusterProfiler R package.

Glycolysis and mitochondrial function were measured by the Seahorses Biosciences XFe analyzer [21]. Macrophages were seeded in complete culture media in the Seahorse BioSciences XFe assay wells. After 24 h, different stimuli (LPS/IFN-γ or LPS/IFN-γ with T-CDs) were added. The glycolytic rate was measured using the Glycolytic Rate Assay Kit (Seahorses Biosciences, 103344). The final concentrations of rotenone/antimycin A and 2-Deoxy-D-Glucose (2-DG) were 0.5 µM and 50 mM respectively according to the protocol of the kit. To measure glycolytic rates, Seahorse XF Glycolytic Rate Assay utilizes both extracellular acidification rate (ECAR) and oxygen consumption rates (OCR) measurements to calculate the glycolytic proton efflux rates (glycoPER) of the cells by the Seahorse XF Glycolytic Rate Assay Report Generators. In terms of mitochondrial respiration, the OCRs in real time was measured using the Cell Mito Stress Kit (Seahorses Biosciences, 103015). The final concentration of Oligomycin was 1.5 µM, the final concentration of FCCP was 2.5 µM, and the final concentration of rotenone/antimycin A was 0.5 µM according to the kit. Data were normalized to cell number on culture plates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rat periodontitis model establishment and treatment
ce:section.ce:para: The animal study was approved by the West China Hospital, Sichuan University (20240625002). Male rats (260–280 g) were purchased from Dashuo Laboratory Animal Technology, Ltd. (Chengdu, China) and were fed standard food and water ad libitum with a 12 h day/night light cycle. The rat experimental periodontitis was established by ligating a 3–0 silk suture around the left maxillary second molar. The ligatures were removed after 21 days, followed by the periodontal injection around the ligated molars every other day for 7 doses (10 μL/dose).

All animals were sacrificed on the day after the last dose of periodontal injection. First, blood samples were collected by eyeball extirpation, which was followed by coagulated at room temperature and centrifuged at 800 g for 10 min. For the RNA extraction of gingiva, the buccal and lingual gingiva tissues of the left maxillary second molars were rinsed in cold PBS, frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored in -80℃. The left half of maxilla, cervical lymph nodes, heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney were dissected and fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde at 4 °C overnight and stored in 70 % ethanol.

The rat left maxillary samples were scanned by a micro-computed tomography system (CT50; SCANCO Medical AG, Switzerland) with a voxel resolution of 10 µm. The three-dimensional reconstruction of the maxilla was built by SCANCO Visualizer software. The bone resorption areas (mm2) of the buccal and lingual views were measured on the reconstructed images by Image J. The bone resorption area (mm2) was defined as the area enclosed by the alveolar bone crest (ABC) of the alveolar bone and the continuous line of cemental-enamel junction (CEJ) of the three molars. The bone volume per tissue volume (BV/TV) at the region of interest (ROI) of the root furcation of the maxillary second molars were analyzed by SCANCO Evaluation software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histologic and immunofluorescent staining of tissues
ce:section.ce:para: Rat maxillae were decalcified in 10 % ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) for 2 months and embedded in paraffin. Cervical lymph nodes and main internal organs were also embedded in paraffin. Sagittal sections of the maxillae were stained for H&E and TRAP staining (387A-1KTF, Sigma-Aldrich; methyl green counterstain). TRAP+ cells were counted as osteoclasts. The sagittal sections of maxillae, cervical lymph nodes and spleen were also probed with anti-CD68 (1:100, GB113109-100, Servicebio), anti-CD163 (1:200, GB15340-100, Servicebio), anti-iNOS (1:200, ab3523, Abcam) for immunohistochemistry analysis. Relative quantification was performed using Image J.

The data in the study were all presented as mean ± standard deviation. We applied the unpaired t test for statistical comparison between two groups, and one-way ANOVA with Tukey’s multiple analysis for multiple groups. The p values were calculated by SPSS 16.0 software, and a value of *p < 0.05 was regarded as significant and **p < 0.01 highly significant.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterized the morphology of T-CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 2
A. T-CDs were spherical-like nanoparticles with an average particle size of 4.5 nm. The high-resolution TEM image revealed the clear lattice fringes in the structure of T-CDs. The lattice spacing was measured to be 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (001) plane of graphite carbon to prove the formation of CDs [22]. These results demonstrated that the T-CDs were strongly carbonized and crystallized with a typical graphene structure. Notably, T-CDs showed poor dispersibility and four or five individual T-CDs were gathered to form a large particle according to the TEM. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of T-CDs were measured to be 213 nm and electrically neutral (Fig. 2 B). The hydrodynamic diameter of T-CDs exhibited a slightly larger size compared to that in TEM, which could be attributed to the existence of various functional groups on the surface of T-CDs. The pathological microenvironment of periodontitis is featured by redox imbalance. In this study, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was added to the solution of T-CDs to simulate the inflammatory environment in vivo, and the changes of T-CDs in particle size, polymer dispersity index (PDI), and zeta potential were measured. It could be seen that after the addition of H2O2, the particle size and PDI of T-CDs showed a significant increase and its zeta potential changed from neutral to negatively charged. These changes might be due to the oxidation of functional groups in the structure of T-CDs, such as the oxidation of hydroxyl groups to carboxyl groups, resulting in the breakdown of the structure of T-CDs.

The optical characteristics of T-CDs were performed via ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescent (FL) spectra analyses (Fig. 2 C). It indicated that T-CDs have a single prominent absorption peak at 420 nm, and the optimal excitation wavelength is 530 nm with the emission wavelength of 420 nm. The redshift (∼110 nm) between excitation wavelength and emission wavelength demonstrated the presence of abundant groups modifying the surface of T-CDs, which endowed them with stability and good water solubility to improve their applicability. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to further investigate the chemical structure of T-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 D, T-CDs were mainly composed of oxygen and carbon, with a small amount of nitrogen. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s (Fig. 2 E) could be deconvolved into three peaks of 531.29 eV, 532.49 eV and 533.29 eV, which were respectively attributed to O-H, C = O and C-O. Three peaks (398.94 eV, 399.84 eV and 401.74 eV) were visible in the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2 F), suggesting the presence of C = N, C-N and N-H. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2 G) shows five peaks: C-C (284.39 eV), C-N (284.94 eV), C-O (286.04 eV), C = N (286.49 eV) and C = O (287.84 eV).

T-CDs were prepared as hydrogel T-GCDs to achieve in-situ injection into the periodontal pockets of rats. T-CDs released from T-GCDs were observed via FL spectra analyses. T-GCDs were added into a dialysis tube, and the fluorescence of the dialysis water outside at different times was measured to investigate the release of T-CDs. It could be seen in Fig. 2 H that obvious fluorescence could be detected in the dialysis water after 16 h and reached its maximum at 72 h. These results indicated that T-CDs could be effectively released from T-GCDs, laying the foundation for therapeutic efficacy in vivo.

The free radical scavenging experiments were conducted to investigate the antioxidant capacity of the T-CDs, and the outcomes were shown in Fig. 2 I. There was a well •OH inhibition efficiency (82.63 %) at the low concentration of T-CDs (0.05 mg/mL). With the increase in concentration, the inhibition of T-CDs to •O2 − was further promoted, and about 40 % of •O2 − was successfully inhibited at the highest concentration (0.8 mg/mL). Similar to the result of •O2 −, the scavenging efficiency of T-CDs showed the concentration dependence, and more than 80 % of DPPH• could be cleared at the concentration of 0.8 mg/mL. These results all suggested the potent antioxidant ability of T-CDs. The cytotoxicity of T-CDs was assessed in macrophages (RAW 264.7) and fibroblasts (L929) and in vitro. CCK-8 and apoptosis analysis showed that high concentration of T-CDs (100 μg/mL) did not affect cell survival and apoptosis after treating for 72 h ( Fig. S1-
2).

Excessive ROS in macrophages is one of the primary causes of periodontal inflammation and tissue destruction [23]. Therefore, we further validated the intracellular ROS scavenging ability of T-CDs in macrophages to evaluate the antioxidant capacity. The ROS probe, CellROX, was applied to investigate the intracellular ROS levels in macrophage RAW 264.7 cells under oxidative stress. 100 μM H2O2 was employed to induce the oxidative stress. The group of untreated macrophages in the normal culture medium was set as the control group. The stimulation of H2O2 elicited the accumulation of intracellular ROS, which was remarkably reduced by the pretreatment of T-CDs (Fig. 3
A-B,
Fig. S3
A). Flow cytometry analysis showed that the pretreatment of T-CDs reduced the percentage of the ROS-positive cells from ∼ 99 % to ∼ 55 % in the stimulation of H2O2 (Fig. 3 C). Inflammatory microenvironment can trigger excessive ROS in macrophages. We then investigated the antioxidant property of T-CDs in lipopolysaccharides (LPS) and IFN-γ stimulation. LPS and IFN-γ caused intensified fluorescent signals of ROS in macrophages according to confocal microscopy images, while the pretreatment of T-CDs reversed the inflammation-triggered accumulation of ROS (Fig. 3 D-E and Fig. S3
B). Flow cytometry analysis showed that the percentage of ROS-positive macrophages stimulated by LPS was ∼ 70 % (Fig. 3 F). In contrast, the ROS positivity was reduced to ∼ 40 % in the T-CDs-pretreated group. These results demonstrated the effectiveness of the T-CDs in scavenging ROS in macrophages under oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immunomodulatory effects of T-CDs on macrophage polarization
ce:section.ce:para:
Macrophages are the main immune cells infiltrating in the periodontal lesion sites, secreting multiple types of inflammatory cytokines and leading to refractory inflammation [5]. Tissue-resident macrophages exhibit functional plasticity by switching into classical phenotype (M1 macrophages) and alternative phenotype (M2 macrophages), thereby modulating outcomes of periodontal inflammation [24]. Thus, we assessed the immunomodulatory functions of T-CDs on macrophage polarization in inflammatory stimulation. According to the results of qRT-PCR (Fig. 4
A), LPS/IFN-γ stimulation induced the M1 macrophage polarization by increasing mRNA expression levels of Nos2, Tnfα, Il6, and decreasing mRNA level of Arg1. The addition of T-CDs significantly reversed the M1 macrophage polarization by reducing the expression of M1-related mRNAs and elevating the M2-related mRNA (Arg1) level. Furthermore, flow cytometry showed LPS/IFN-γ induced a dramatic increase of CD86+ macrophages (M1-specific marker) to 83 %, and the addition of T-CDs reduced the percentage to 70 % (Fig. 3 B-C). Compared with the inflammatory stimulus of LPS/IFN-γ, the treatment of T-CDs also significantly increased the rate of CD206+ macrophages (M2-specific marker) to 60 % under the stimulation of LPS/IFN-γ (Fig. 4 D-E). Immunofluorescent staining of macrophages further validated that the T-CDs dramatically inhibited the expression of iNOS (M1-specific marker) and enhanced the expression of CD206 (M2-specific marker) under the LPS/IFN-γ-triggered inflammatory condition (Fig. 4 F-H,
Fig. S4 ). These results confirmed the immunomodulatory functions of T-CDs to promote M2 macrophage polarization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: T-CDs rebalance glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration in macrophages
ce:section.ce:para:
To get insights into the potential mechanisms of the immunomodulatory functions of T-CDs on macrophages, we performed RNA-seq to monitor the transcriptomic changes in macrophages treated by T-CDs under the inflammatory condition in vitro (Fig. 5
A-B). Compared with the LPS/IFN-γ-stimulated group, the treatment of T-CDs down-regulated M1-related genes, such as Il1b, Nos2, Il6, Hif1a and Cxcl9, and upregulated M2-related genes, such as Csf1r, Mrc1, Trib1, Cd5l and Tgfb1. We also observed the downregulated genes involved in glutathione synthesis (Gstt1, Gstt3, Gstm1) in the T-CDs treated group, indicating decreased ROS generation and alleviating oxidative stress [25]. Gene ontology (GO) enrichment analysis of the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) revealed enriched biological process terms closely associated with inflammation in the LPS/IFN-γ group compared with the T-CDs group, such as inflammatory response, responses to bacterium and cytokine secretion (Fig. 5 C).

Metabolic reprogramming plays a critical role in the activation and phenotypic shift of macrophages [26]. According to previous evidence, M1 macrophages display metabolic reprogramming toward aerobic glycolysis, while M2 macrophage polarization is featured by mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) [27]. The above RNA-seq results showed that T-CDs significantly down-regulated genes involved in glucose uptake and glycolysis, such as Slc2a, Pfkfb3, Gpi1 and Aldoc (Fig. 5 A). Furthermore, the Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway analysis (Fig. 5 D) showed enriched HIF-1 signaling pathway in the LPS/IFN-γ group compared with the T-CDs group, which was closed to glycolysis. The Reactome Enrichment analysis (Fig. 5 E) further unveiled the enriched term of glycolysis in the LPS/IFN-γ group compared with the T-CDs group. Therefore, T-CDs inhibited the glycolysis of macrophages under the inflammatory stimulus. Given the interrelationship between glycolysis and mitochondrial OXPHO in energy metabolism, T-CDs might help against glycolysis by restoring the mitochondrial function of macrophages in the inflammatory condition.

In order to verify our assumption, the mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm) of macrophages was monitored with JC-1, which is the global indicator of mitochondrial function. An amount of fluorescence probe JC-1 will aggregate at mitochondria with a high ΔΨm and then emit the red fluorescent signal, indicating the physiological mitochondrial state. On the contrary, when ΔΨm values of mitochondria decrease, JC-1 will exist as a monomer and emit a green fluorescence signal, which is a hallmark of mitochondrial dysfunction [28]. As shown in Fig. 6
A, the macrophages treated with LPS/IFN-γ displayed mitochondrial dysfunction with highly fluorescent intensity of monomers (green) and weak signals of aggregates (red). The T-CDs treatment significantly led to increased JC-1 aggregates and decreased monomers (green). According to the quantitative analysis for Fig. 6 A, the elevated ratio of aggregates to monomers in the T-CDs group was much higher than that of the LPS/IFN-γ group ( Fig. S5 ). These results all suggested that T-CDs could restore the mitochondrial function of macrophages.

Furthermore, we performed the Seahorse assay to investigate the glycolysis and mitochondrial respiration in macrophages treated with different groups [29–31]. The glycolytic proton efflux rates (glycoPER) in real-time were firstly measured by the extrusion of protons, which were produced by lactate and applied as the measures of glycolysis. It was shown in Fig. 6 B and Fig. 6 D, the T-CDs group showed both reduced basal glycolytic rate and compensatory glycolytic rate. While the real-time cellular oxygen consumption rates (OCR) were monitored with inhibitors of specific substrate oxidation pathways to evaluate mitochondrial respiration. The T-CDs group demonstrated increased OCR in basal respiration, maximal respiration and ATP production (Fig. 6 C, E). Therefore, inhibiting glycolysis and restoring mitochondrial function are the potential mechanisms of the immunomodulatory effect of T-CDs on macrophages (Fig. 6 F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogel T-GCDs applied in the treatment of periodontitis in vivo
The therapeutic effects of T-GCDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
To validate the therapeutic effect of T-CDs on periodontitis in vivo, we established a ligature-induced experimental periodontitis rat model (Fig. 7
A), which has similar pathological features to human periodontitis during inflammatory and healing phases [32]. 3–0 silk ligatures were tied around the right maxillary second molars for 21 days to induce periodontitis [4]. The ligatures were then removed, and T-GCDs were in-situ injected into the periodontal pocket (10 μL) every 2 days for 7 doses [33]. These rats were randomly sorted into 3 groups (n = 6 per group): i) healthy rats with sham operation (control group/CON); ii) rats with periodontitis treated by phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) injection (periodontitis group/P); iii) rats with periodontitis followed by the injection of T-GCDs (T-GCDs group/T-GCDs). After the treatments, the alveolar bone, gingiva, serum and main internal organs were harvested for further investigation.

Periodontitis is characterized by the resorption of alveolar bone, leading to the reduced height and density of alveolar bone [34]. The harvested alveolar bones of rats were scanned with the micro-CT. As shown in the three-dimensional reconstruction in Fig. 7 B, it could be observed that compared to the control group, the bone resorption marked with red areas between the cemental-enamel junction (CEJ) and alveolar bone crest (ABC) spread from the first to the third molars in the periodontitis group. After the local injection of T-CDs, the areas between the CEJ and ABC showed a significant reduction, indicating the alleviation of alveolar bone loss. The therapeutic effect of T-CDs was also confirmed by the quantitative analysis of the micro-CT results, which showed increased trabecular bone volume per tissue volume (BV/TV) (Fig. 7 C) and decreased alveolar bone loss area (Fig. 7 D) compared with periodontitis group. Furthermore, H&E staining revealed that the ligatures caused alveolar bone destruction, which was rescued by local injection of T-CDs (Fig. 7 E). Tartrate-resistant acid phosphatase (TRAP) staining showed the injection of T-CDs significantly reduced the number of TRAP+ osteoclasts (black arrows) compared with periodontitis (Fig. 7 F, G). Furthermore, qRT-PCR analysis of gingiva around the second maxillary molar also confirmed reduced expression level of M1-related genes (Il1b, Nos2) and elevated expression level of M2-related genes (Arg1, Mrc1) in T-GCDs group compared with periodontitis group (Fig. 7 H), indicating the anti-inflammatory effect of T-GCDs on periodontitis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The therapeutic mechanism of T-GCDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para:
To further validate the in vivo immunomodulatory function of T-GCDs, we applied immunofluorescent staining of periodontal and immune tissues to observe macrophage phenotypes. Pan-macrophages (Mφ) were stained by the marker of CD68 with red fluorescence, while M1 and M2 macrophages were dividedly labeled by the M1-related marker (iNOS) and M2-related marker (CD163) with green fluorescence signal. As shown in Fig. 8
A and Fig. 8 B, periodontitis increased the number of CD68+iNOS+ double-stained cells and decreased the number of CD68+CD163+ double-stained cells, which indicated the M1 macrophage polarization in periodontitis. Compared to the periodontitis group, periodontal injection of T-GCDs led to reduced number of CD68+iNOS+ M1 macrophages and increased the number of CD68+CD163+ M2 macrophages, suggesting the promotion of M2 macrophage polarization by T-GCDs. Similarly, the effect of T-GCDs to enhance M2 macrophage polarization was also observed in rat cervical lymph nodes (CLNs) and spleen (Fig. 8 C-D,
Fig. S7 ).

We then performed RNA-seq of gingiva around the second maxillary molars from different groups to analyze biological changes at the high-throughput level (Fig. 9 ). The differential gene heatmap showed that compared with the healthy rats, periodontitis reduced the expression anti-inflammatory genes (Arg1, Mrc1, Il10, Tgfb1) and promoted the expression of pro-inflammatory genes (Il33, Il36a, Cxcl9, Cxcl17) (Fig. 9 A). The local injection of T-GCDs reversed the changes of above-mentioned genes in gingiva, suggesting the anti-inflammatory effect of T-CDs in vivo. Notably, the T-GCDs group led to decreased expression of glycolysis-related genes (Pdk4, Eno3, Ugp2), which is in line with effect of T-CDs on glycolysis in vitro. The GO enrichment analysis revealed enriched term related with inflammation in periodontitis compared with T-GCDs group, such as inflammatory response, response to bacterium, cellular response to lipopolysaccharide (Fig. 9 B). KEGG analysis also showed enriched inflammation-related pathways in periodontitis group compared with T-GCDs group, such as PI3K-AKT signaling pathway and cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction (Fig. 9 C). Overall, these in vivo results suggested that T-GCDs exhibit potent therapeutical effects on inflammation resolution in periodontitis.

This study firstly reported the CDs-based strategy to transform Chinese medicine Dracocephalum moldavica L. into nanomedicine T-CDs through one-step solvothermal approach, which endowed T-CDs with excellent water solubility and biocompatibility. T-CDs exhibited potent free radical and intracellular ROS scavenging abilities, and the hydrogel prepared by T-CDs showed considerable resolution of periodontal inflammation and reparation of damaged tissues in periodontitis. According to the further investigations, T-CDs displayed robust immunomodulatory effect to promote M2 macrophage polarization in vitro and in vivo. Rebalancing inflammation-induced glycolysis towards mitochondrial respiration of macrophages has been proved to be the potential mechanism of the immunomodulatory effect of T-CDs. Our findings highlight the therapeutic potential of the T-CDs for effective immunomodulation in treating inflammatory disorders like periodontitis and provide a promising therapeutic nanoplatform to utilize Chinese medicine for wide biomedical applications.

Bo Zhang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yifei Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhongshan He: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation. Jie Fang: Writing – review & editing. Mengran Guo: Writing – review & editing. Xinchun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zhihe Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiangrong Song: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.
